html:
Ryoyo Ryosan Holdings, Inc. (TSE:167A)

Ryoyo Ryosan is not the absurd 2 to 3x earnings stock suggested by the stale prior draft. The company is smaller, cleaner, and more complicated: FY2026 revenue was ¥359.9bn, operating profit was ¥10.1bn, net income was ¥7.4bn, and book value was ¥3,413 per share. At the May 18 close of ¥2,680, the stock trades at 0.79x book, a 5.2% dividend yield, and roughly 14.4x reported EPS, with no FY2027 earnings guidance because Renesas has requested termination of a distributor agreement covering ¥84.2bn of FY2025 sales, or 23.4% of consolidated revenue.

That Renesas notice is the whole setup. The market reaction has treated it like a structural break in the franchise. The stock fell from the low ¥3,000s to the high ¥2,000s and printed a new 2026 low on May 15. I think that is too blunt. Distributor revenue is not software revenue. Losing 23% of sales does not mean losing 23% of profit, especially in a business where gross margin is near 10% and device segment operating margin was only 2.25% in FY2026. If Renesas product flow was roughly segment-average, the direct operating-profit exposure is closer to ¥1.5bn to ¥2.5bn, before mitigation, cost actions, substitute line-card sales, and customer retention. That is serious, but not remotely fatal against ¥10.1bn of operating profit, ¥136.9bn of net assets, and a still-solvent balance sheet.

The more interesting point is that the company was already improving before the Renesas shock. In FY2026, sales were flat, but gross profit rose from ¥34.9bn to ¥37.6bn and operating profit rose 18.6%. Q4 alone generated about ¥3.4bn of operating profit, a 3.4% operating margin on roughly ¥101bn of sales. The device business declined 1.9% in revenue but increased segment profit 27.9% as the mix shifted toward more profitable products. The solutions business grew 5.0% in revenue and 19.9% in segment profit, now producing ¥4.37bn of profit on ¥105.3bn of revenue. That is the hidden transition: the market still labels this as a low-return Japanese semiconductor trading house, while the profit mix is migrating toward AI infrastructure, GPU servers, embedded boards, security, robotics, factory automation, and integration work.

The company is explicitly pushing in that direction. It says demand for generative AI, cloud, and IT infrastructure should remain firm in FY2027 even as device demand recovers only gradually. Its AI Techmate program had over 100 generated projects and 7 orders at the half-year stage. It also formed a capital and business alliance with Retriever to combine GPU infrastructure, networking, LLM implementation, and agentic AI deployment for Japanese enterprises. In April, the merged operating company and NTT demonstrated industrial robot remote control over IOWN APN, with potential installation work reductions of about 30%, and in some post-installation work, about 50%. This is not yet a large earnings stream, but it gives Ryoyo Ryosan a credible route away from being valued as a replaceable pass-through distributor.

The balance sheet gives the company time. Cash was ¥36.0bn at March 2026 against interest-bearing debt and commercial paper of roughly ¥47bn, so net debt is modest. Working capital is large, but it is mostly receivables and inventory tied to electronics distribution. Book value per share of ¥3,413 means the current price already discounts a meaningful impairment. The company also maintained the FY2027 dividend at ¥140 per share despite withholding earnings guidance. That is a 5.2% yield at today’s price. The dividend is not bulletproof if Renesas fully exits and management cannot replace the economics, but maintaining it immediately after the Renesas notice is an important signal.

There is also a capital-return nuance the screen may miss. On May 14, the board approved cancellation of 4.0m treasury shares, 7.41% of pre-cancellation issued shares, leaving 50.0m issued shares and about 9.9m treasury shares after cancellation. That does not materially change economic EPS because treasury shares were already excluded from per-share calculations, but it simplifies the capital structure and shows management is willing to reduce stale treasury overhang. The better move would be an actual buyback below book, but even the cancellation helps the rerating story.

The bear argument is obvious: Renesas could fully walk, customers tied to Renesas parts could leave with it, and management’s FY2028 ambition of ¥500bn sales and ¥30bn operating profit could become irrelevant. That is why the stock is cheap on book but not screaming cheap on current earnings. The FY2026 net income also included securities-sale gains, so recurring earnings power was lower than reported EPS suggests. This is not a no-brainer net-net. It is an event-driven rerating bet where the market has probably overcapitalized the worst headline and undercapitalized the mix shift.

The variant perception is that Renesas is not only a loss risk, it is also a forced strategic reset. A lower-quality, low-margin principal line may shrink just as the combined company is finally integrating sales, IT, AI infrastructure, robotics, embedded-board, and security capabilities. If management can stabilize the Renesas transition and show FY2027 operating profit still near ¥8bn to ¥11bn, the stock should stop trading as a broken distributor. If it can show solutions growth and device margin improvement at the same time, investors will start valuing it as a small Japanese AI/industrial-infrastructure reseller-integrator with a book-value floor and a 5% yield.

Current price: ¥2,680.

Bear case: ¥1,900, down 29%. Renesas revenue is substantially lost, operating profit falls toward ¥5bn, EPS drops below ¥100, and the dividend is cut. The stock trades at about 0.55x book.

Base case: ¥3,700, up 38%. Renesas impact is material but manageable, FY2027 to FY2028 net income normalizes around ¥8bn to ¥9bn, EPS is roughly ¥200 to ¥225, and the stock rerates to about 1.1x book while paying the ¥140 dividend.

Bull case: ¥6,500, up 143%. Renesas disruption is negotiated or replaced faster than feared, solutions and AI infrastructure scale, device margins continue recovering, and operating profit moves toward ¥20bn to ¥25bn, still below the company’s ¥30bn medium-term goal. At 12 to 14x bull-case EPS, the stock more than doubles.
